articles.title: Weather Update: North India Braces for Heatwave as Floods Devastate Northeast - Rozana Spokesman
articles.full_text:
The crisis extends into Assam, where floodwaters have inundated six districts.
North India is currently bracing for a period of sweltering weather as a temporary break in monsoon activity leads to rising temperatures. Over the last three days, states including Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, and the city of Chandigarh have faced intense heat and stifling humidity. In the capital, mercury levels reached 38 degrees Celsius on Tuesday, signaling a challenging week ahead for residents across the region.
The India Meteorological Department has issued a forecast indicating that hot and humid conditions will persist through July 16 for Punjab, Haryana, Delhi, and several other states. As the primary rain-bearing winds remain subdued, citizens are encouraged to prioritize hydration and minimize outdoor exposure during peak afternoon hours. This weather pattern highlights the volatility of the current monsoon season.
In sharp contrast to the dry heat in the north, other parts of the country are battling severe natural disasters. Arunachal Pradesh has been hit hard by heavy rainfall, leading to widespread flooding and landslides that have damaged numerous homes. The situation is critical, with authorities reporting that over 102,000 people have been affected, resulting in seven tragic fatalities and multiple injuries across the state.
The crisis extends into Assam, where floodwaters have inundated six districts. Currently, nearly 100 villages are submerged, impacting over 37,000 residents. Lakhimpur has emerged as the worst-hit area, bearing the brunt of the rising waters. Meanwhile, an orange alert has been issued for Odisha and Bengal due to the risk of heavy rain and thunderstorms.
While the northeast manages flood relief efforts and other coastal states prepare for high winds, North India awaits a much-needed meteorological shift. Forecasters expect the current dry spell to break later this week, potentially bringing cooler temperatures and relief from the present humidity. Until then, the nation remains under a complex weather alert, balancing between extreme heat and monsoon-driven floods.
